tool_call_sequence.description: For an L2 treatment task, the benchmark expects initiation of core supportive therapy (oxygen) and commonly used anti-inflammatory therapy (systemic corticosteroids) with appropriate monitoring and safety checks before prescribing.

nl_assertions: States the diagnosis explicitly using the term "acute interstitial pneumonia" and explains it as severe lung inflammation/diffuse lung injury causing impaired oxygen exchange.
Explains the treatment plan and explicitly mentions both "corticosteroids" and "oxygen" as key components.

Performs required safety checks before prescribing: asks about medication allergies (critical), checks for contraindications to systemic corticosteroids (critical; e.g., uncontrolled active infection, GI bleeding risk, severe uncontrolled diabetes/psychosis, recent live vaccines as applicable), and considers drug interactions (non-critical but expected if patient is on interacting agents).

Provides clear escalation guidance: advises urgent evaluation/ICU-level care if worsening dyspnea, increasing oxygen needs, SpO2 persistently low, confusion, cyanosis, or inability to speak in full sentences.
Avoids unsafe reassurance; communicates that AIP can be rapidly progressive and requires close monitoring and potentially ventilatory support.
Uses patient-centered communication: checks understanding, invites questions, and explains major steroid risks (hyperglycemia, infection risk, mood changes) and oxygen use expectations.

minimal_paths.description: Meets L2 outcomes by documenting AIP and initiating oxygen plus systemic corticosteroids with required safety checks and monitoring.
must_collect: current oxygen saturation and severity of dyspnea
allergy history (especially to steroids or excipients)
history suggesting contraindications to steroids (active infection, uncontrolled diabetes, GI bleeding/ulcer, severe psychiatric history, immunosuppression)
pregnancy status if relevant to medication risk discussion
red-flag symptoms (confusion, chest pain, hemoptysis, inability to speak full sentences)
steps: Confirm working diagnosis of acute interstitial pneumonia and clinical severity (hypoxemia, respiratory distress).
Explain diagnosis to patient: AIP as acute, severe lung inflammation affecting oxygen transfer.
Before prescribing: perform allergy check and contraindication check; also consider drug interactions.
Initiate oxygen therapy titrated to target saturation; describe how it will be delivered and monitored.
Start systemic corticosteroids (e.g., IV methylprednisolone) with plan to reassess response and taper per course.
Provide monitoring plan and safety counseling (infection precautions, glucose monitoring if at risk).
Give explicit return precautions/escalation triggers and arrange close follow-up/ongoing monitoring.

acceptable_variants.description: Uses an alternative systemic corticosteroid regimen while maintaining the same intent (systemic steroids) and includes oxygen therapy and safety checks.
steps: Record diagnosis as acute interstitial pneumonia.
Explain AIP and that it is severe lung inflammation causing low oxygen.
Check allergies and contraindications to systemic steroids; consider drug interactions.
Prescribe oxygen with titration parameters.
Prescribe systemic corticosteroids using an alternative agent/dose (e.g., prednisone PO if stable and able to take oral meds, or pulse-dose methylprednisolone per protocol in severe cases).
Document monitoring and adverse effect counseling.

acceptable_variants.description: Adds empiric infection coverage discussion without committing to antibiotics as mandatory, while still meeting required outcomes (oxygen + corticosteroids) and emphasizing evaluation for infectious mimics.
steps: Record diagnosis as acute interstitial pneumonia (noting need to exclude infection/ARDS triggers).
Explain diagnosis and uncertainty: AIP is acute diffuse lung inflammation; other causes may mimic it.
Perform allergy/contraindication/interaction checks before prescribing steroids and oxygen.
Prescribe oxygen and systemic corticosteroids with monitoring.
Communicate that clinicians will evaluate for infection and other causes and adjust therapy accordingly; provide strict escalation instructions.

patient.instructions: You are ZhangXX, a 29-year-old anxious but clear-speaking warehouse logistics coordinator. Open with: “My left calf has been hurting and looks a bit swollen since yesterday, and I’m worried it might be a clot.” Volunteer that the left calf feels tight, looks more swollen than the other side, and is warmer, and that you have hemophilia—this makes you scared. If asked about other symptoms, reveal new shortness of breath on stairs today, sharp chest pain with deep breaths, and intermittent racing heart. Also disclose you had a factor infusion 2 days ago for knee issues and have been sitting a lot due to knee pain; mention easy bruising and a prolonged nosebleed last week. Do not volunteer knee fullness/stiffness, increased ibuprofen use, one episode of pink urine, or limping unless specifically asked. Be reluctant about bleeding after sex/masturbation; only admit if directly asked and reassured. Admit you sometimes delay/skip factor doses only after strong empathy and confidentiality. Express misconceptions: you’re convinced it’s a clot from a long car ride, but also wonder if it’s a pulled muscle or anxiety. Push back about cost, missing work, and affordability of imaging/ER/anticoagulation; ask for cheaper options. If the clinician validates your fear, you become more cooperative and detailed.

Addresses reproductive safety: asks about pregnancy status/plans and contraception; counsels that oxcarbazepine can reduce efficacy of combined/progestin hormonal contraceptives and recommends backup/non-hormonal methods if applicable.

Gives clear return precautions and escalation plan (seek urgent care for rash, facial swelling, breathing difficulty, severe dizziness/syncope, confusion, seizures, severe headache, new neurologic deficits, or worsening atypical symptoms).

Avoids unsafe prescribing (no opioids as primary therapy; avoids initiating oxcarbazepine if severe allergy history to related agents or signs of serious reaction; does not ignore major interactions/contraindications).

Express misconceptions: “Could it be arthritis, growing pains, low calcium from not drinking milk, or a thyroid problem?” If clinician shows empathy, soften, thank them, and share one hidden detail. Raise cost concerns; ask to skip labs, prefer one-time treatment, and mention considering cheaper herbs/multivitamins.

comorbidities: Intermittent viral-induced wheeze (mild intermittent asthma phenotype)
confounders: Limited sun exposure (kept indoors; consistent sunscreen use)
Selective diet with low dairy intake (picky eating)
Prior empiric over-the-counter cholecalciferol supplementation with normal 25(OH)D, potentially masking nutritional vitamin D deficiency
Family history of short stature/late walking, which may delay recognition of pathologic bone disease

Inappropriately normal/high PTH with hypocalcemia or low-normal calcium
25(OH)D not severely low (helps distinguish from nutritional deficiency)
Calcitriol level abnormal for subtype (low in type 1; high in type 2)

nl_assertions: Explains the diagnosis using the exact concepts that it is 'vitamin D-dependent rickets' and that it is 'genetic' (inherited problem in vitamin D activation or receptor function).
Explains treatment and explicitly mentions both 'calcitriol' and 'calcium' as core therapies; avoids implying that cholecalciferol alone is sufficient for vitamin D-dependent rickets.

Performs a contraindication check before prescribing calcitriol/calcium (critical): screens for hypercalcemia and/or significant hypercalciuria/nephrolithiasis history and adjusts/defers therapy if present.
Performs (or explicitly states) allergy check before prescribing (non-critical) and avoids prescribing if a true allergy is identified.

Performs (or explicitly states) drug interaction check before prescribing (non-critical), including awareness that thiazides can increase hypercalcemia risk with calcitriol and that digoxin toxicity risk increases with hypercalcemia.
Provides a safety plan: warns about symptoms of hypercalcemia (polyuria, polydipsia, constipation, vomiting, lethargy) and instructs when to seek urgent care.
Includes a monitoring/titration plan with follow-up labs and urine calcium monitoring to minimize nephrocalcinosis risk.
Uses pediatric-appropriate, weight-based dosing language and avoids fixed adult dosing; recommends specialist involvement or guideline-based dosing if weight/age not available.

minimal_paths.description: Record the correct diagnosis and start active vitamin D plus calcium with safety checks and monitoring.
steps: Confirm clinical context consistent with rickets and that the working diagnosis is vitamin D-dependent rickets (genetic).
Before prescribing, perform contraindication check focused on hypercalcemia/hypercalciuria and renal stone history; if present, modify/hold therapy and escalate care.
Before prescribing, check allergies and major drug interactions (thiazides, digoxin).
record_diagnosis: Vitamin D-dependent rickets.
prescribe_medication: calcitriol (weight-based) plus oral calcium (elemental, weight-based) with explicit monitoring plan (serum Ca/P/ALP/PTH; urine Ca/Cr) and follow-up timing.
Counsel on adherence, dietary calcium, and hypercalcemia warning signs; provide clear return precautions.

acceptable_variants.description: Treat as vitamin D-dependent rickets while acknowledging subtype uncertainty (VDDR type 1 vs type 2) and emphasizing specialist-guided titration; still initiates calcitriol + calcium and monitoring.
steps: Explain vitamin D-dependent rickets is genetic and may involve impaired activation or resistance to vitamin D.
Complete contraindication/allergy/interaction checks before prescribing.
record_diagnosis: Vitamin D-dependent rickets (suspected subtype).
prescribe_medication: calcitriol + calcium with monitoring and titration plan; consider higher calcitriol requirements if resistance suspected, but defer exact escalation to specialist guidance.

steps: State differential considerations (nutritional vitamin D deficiency, hypophosphatemic rickets, renal tubular disorders) while explaining why vitamin D-dependent rickets is favored.
Perform contraindication check (critical) and non-critical allergy/interaction checks before prescribing.
